title: High-Risk Geography Card-Present Card Used for Remote CNP Purchases
narrative: A Visa credit card with historical usage exclusively in Nigeria (high CNP fraud jurisdiction) suddenly processes online purchases from US-based e-commerce merchants. The cardholder's P_emaildomain shows Nigerian provider (e.g., yahoo.com.ng), but billing addr1 claims Florida zip code with dist1 distance of 8,400+ km from email registration location. Transactions cluster at digital goods merchants (ProductCD=W) totaling $2,100-$4,800 across 6-9 purchases within 72 hours. All transactions occur during US business hours (txn_hour 9-17) despite card's prior transaction history showing consistent African timezone patterns (txn_hour 20-04 UTC). Card1_txn_count_24h jumps from baseline 0.3 to 7, with card1_amt_zscore exceeding 4.2. AVS returns partial match (Z-code) and CVV passes, suggesting card details harvested but not physical possession.
indicators: Card historical usage 100% in Nigeria or similar high-risk CNP jurisdiction (last 90 days)
Sudden shift to US merchant MCCs with dist1 exceeding 8,000 km from P_emaildomain registration country
6+ digital goods transactions (ProductCD=W) totaling $2,100-$4,800 in 48-72 hour window
Transaction timezone misalignment: txn_hour 9-17 UTC contradicts cardholder's historical 20-04 pattern
card1_txn_count_24h spike from baseline <1 to 6+, card1_amt_zscore >4.0
AVS mismatch (partial Z-code) with billing addr1 in different continent than P_emaildomain origin
R_emaildomain absent or differs from established P_emaildomain country suffix
summary: A card with exclusive Nigerian transaction history suddenly makes multiple US online purchases for digital goods, with billing address in Florida but email domain registered in Nigeria. Transaction timing shifts from African nighttime patterns to US business hours, while purchase frequency spikes from less than one to six-plus transactions per day.
reasoning: Fraudsters have obtained card details without physical possession and are racing to extract value through hard-to-reverse digital goods before the legitimate cardholder notices. The geographic contradictions, timezone shifts, and velocity spikes indicate the card is being used remotely by someone other than the genuine owner who established the original usage patterns.
indicator_explanations: Card has only been used in a high-fraud country for months
Purchase suddenly appears thousands of miles from the email's registered location
Burst of digital goods purchases racks up thousands in two days
Transactions happen during hours the cardholder has never shopped before
Card goes from rarely used to hammered with elevated spending amounts
Billing address is on a different continent than the email domain origin
Recipient email missing or from different country than purchaser's email
